# Questions in Livestream 96

Live Stream #96: Like A Goodwill Shop Exploded

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction |
|---:|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pfU_HsxRrV4?t=320" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">5:20</a> | Which ancient Egyptian custom would he most like to bring back today? | He picked the family cemetery picnic tradition that kept the dead present within family memory. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pfU_HsxRrV4?t=448" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">7:28</a> | How did ordinary Egyptians perceive corruption, and did it weaken faith in the pharaonic system? | He said corruption was systemic and ordinary people participated in it; loss of royal power and status mattered more than corruption for weakening the institution. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pfU_HsxRrV4?t=666" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">11:06</a> | Who is to blame for linking Amenhotep II with 1446 BCE? | He pointed to James Orr's 1906 book as the source that popularized the theory. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pfU_HsxRrV4?t=778" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">12:58</a> | If one were a biblical minimalist, which discoveries from the last fifty years would most push toward maximalism? | He named the Tel Dan Stele as the major discovery because it shifted debate from whether David existed to the scope of David's kingdom and influence. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pfU_HsxRrV4?t=906" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">15:06</a> | Why are ancient Near Eastern grammars outside Hebrew and Aramaic often so high-context? | He distinguished introductory grammars from dense reference grammars, which are designed for detailed lookup and often assume an instructor. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pfU_HsxRrV4?t=1294" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">21:34</a> | Who was the first Egyptian, and where did the term "Egyptian" derive from? | He said the first Egyptian is impossible to identify, while "Egypt" comes through Greek Aigyptos from Coptos, not from an Egyptian self-designation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pfU_HsxRrV4?t=1452" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">24:12</a> | Should Genesis 2's dust and rib be read literally or archetypically? | He favored a middle-ground analogical reading: not flattened into strict literalism, but not treated as merely figurative either. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pfU_HsxRrV4?t=1670" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">27:50</a> | Did the Law of Moses influence later Greek philosophers such as Plato on their beliefs about God and the divine? | He said Plato and earlier Greek philosophers were too early for Septuagint influence, though later Hellenistic poets and thinkers could be influenced. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pfU_HsxRrV4?t=1858" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">30:58</a> | What makes him certain that Teru of Urkesh is Terah, father of Abraham? | He cited converging name, place, chronology, geography, and biography parallels as collectively compelling evidence. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pfU_HsxRrV4?t=2833" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">47:13</a> | How can literary parallels be distinguished between intertextuality and coincidence? | He said certainty is often impossible, but intertextuality is usually the safer scholarly explanation because ideas circulate through a culture's shared pool of ideas. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pfU_HsxRrV4?t=3129" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">52:09</a> | Are the differences between 1 Kings 4:26 and 2 Chronicles 9:25 an example of forty being used as a cultural idiom? | He said an idiomatic reading is possible, but a scribal issue or uncertainty over the meaning of "thousand" or division is also possible. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pfU_HsxRrV4?t=3553" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">59:13</a> | In 2 Kings 17:9, what were the secret things Israel did against the Lord? | He connected the phrase to secret religious practices and said the context points to illicit high places, Asherah poles, incense, and idols alongside Yahweh worship. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pfU_HsxRrV4?t=3983" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:06:23</a> | Could tehom, the deep in Babylon, be a better candidate than Tiamat? | He said it is possible and he would not rule it out, but it would require checking the Akkadian etymology. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pfU_HsxRrV4?t=4133" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:08:53</a> | What is the furthest land ancient Egyptians likely knew about? | He said Egyptian horizons included Greece, Italy, Spain, Punt, Mesopotamia, and possibly trade connections reaching India or China, though direct awareness varied. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pfU_HsxRrV4?t=4602" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:16:42</a> | Could the term "matah" for rod or staff be of Egyptian origin, and would that support an early Exodus account? | He said it is possibly an Egyptian loanword, and that such loanwords can support an early core to the Exodus tradition. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pfU_HsxRrV4?t=4738" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:18:58</a> | If the case for the Exodus is strong, why do most scholars reject that it happened as described in the Bible? | He attributed much of the rejection to scholarly tradition, biblical minimalism, and distorted "Sunday school" readings of what the text actually claims. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pfU_HsxRrV4?t=5114" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:25:14</a> | How were stones cut, engineered, and placed to form the Great Pyramid with limited technology? | He pointed to bronze saws and chisels, visible tool marks, rough placement before final finishing, and casing stones rather than aliens or exotic technology. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pfU_HsxRrV4?t=5434" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:30:34</a> | What is his favorite way to have onions for the day of chewing onions for Bast? | He named fried onions and onion rings, green onions in soup or ramen, and onions in beef stroganoff. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pfU_HsxRrV4?t=5660" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:34:20</a> | Who is Menes, and how much did medieval Egyptians know about ancient Egypt? | He said Menes is usually identified with either Aha or Narmer, while later Egyptians retained very little reliable knowledge of ancient Egyptian history and writing. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pfU_HsxRrV4?t=6130" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:42:10</a> | What is the symbolic meaning of destroying high places and Asherah poles, and what would a modern equivalent be? | He treated the destruction as suppressing self-styled worship outside the authorized Yahweh cult, with only imperfect modern analogies. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pfU_HsxRrV4?t=6620" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:50:20</a> | What are his thoughts on Catalhoyuk and its potential interaction with the biblical world, before the flood or Tower of Babel? | He said it is outside his main field, but his tentative view is that it is post-flood and pre-Babel, with no direct interaction with the biblical text. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pfU_HsxRrV4?t=6880" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:54:40</a> | In Genesis 39, how would Joseph have demonstrated his ability in Potiphar's house? | He said Joseph likely showed aptitude through household tasks, literacy, accounting, and administration, with larger tasks serving as practical tests. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pfU_HsxRrV4?t=7228" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:00:28</a> | What advice would he give to someone looking for a church? | He recommended checking the doctrinal statement, ensuring essentials are present, deciding whether secondary issues are livable, and asking whether one can serve there. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pfU_HsxRrV4?t=7915" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:11:55</a> | How fluid is the Old Testament text, and do the Dead Sea Scrolls prove the text was fluid? | He warned that "fluid" is loaded language and said the variants show long copying history and human errors, not necessarily arbitrary corruption. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pfU_HsxRrV4?t=8372" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:19:32</a> | Is ha-satan in Job the devil or an anonymous divine-council accuser? | He said ha-satan is a job function, not a name, and while certainty is lacking, he leans toward identifying this rogue ad hoc council figure with the devil. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pfU_HsxRrV4?t=8709" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:25:09</a> | If the Apocrypha was not intended as Scripture, why was it included in the Septuagint? | He said the Septuagint took a broad, exhaustive "kitchen sink" approach to Jewish texts and should not be treated as a canon list. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pfU_HsxRrV4?t=9044" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:30:44</a> | Has the Cochin Torah been studied for Old Testament textual criticism? | He was not aware of its use in textual criticism, but said an ancient textual tradition there could present an opportunity for future work. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pfU_HsxRrV4?t=9352" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:35:52</a> | Who created death? | He said death, understood as separation, is the result of human sin and disobedience. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pfU_HsxRrV4?t=9535" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:38:55</a> | Why do scholars reject two million Israelites in the Exodus and translate elef as clan rather than 600,000 men? | He said the two million figure is a theological extrapolation and favored genre-based military-number inflation over translating elef as clan. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pfU_HsxRrV4?t=10141" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:49:01</a> | Are there corruptions in the Septuagint, Old Testament forgeries, or historical errors in Deuteronomy and Daniel? | He allowed copyist errors and pseudepigrapha, but rejected calling Deuteronomy or Daniel forgeries and said Daniel is better explained by recontextualization. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pfU_HsxRrV4?t=10745" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:59:05</a> | Has he examined the claims of the Kemetic community? | He said he has examined both Kemetic Orthodox and conscious-community claims, while noting that the two communities are very different. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pfU_HsxRrV4?t=10898" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:01:38</a> | Does he have a position on documented Eucharistic miracles? | He evaluates miracle claims case by case; some may be valid, some not, and he does not see the phenomena as exclusive to the Eucharist. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pfU_HsxRrV4?t=11275" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:07:55</a> | What would he say to a Christian friend who keeps Jewish dietary laws out of legalism, obedience, or a way to get closer to God? | He distinguished cultural practice from legalism, saying dietary practice for culture can be acceptable but treating it as required for righteousness compromises grace. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pfU_HsxRrV4?t=11966" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:19:26</a> | What is the earliest chronological time mentioned in the Old Testament that can be confirmed? | He said it depends on whether "confirmed" means event or date, naming Terah's sojourn as an early confirmable event and Josiah's death as a firm dated event. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pfU_HsxRrV4?t=12398" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:26:38</a> | Is there extrabiblical evidence for Hoshea, the last king of Israel? | He was not sure and said he would have to look it up, while noting a viewer's suggested lead about a bulla. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pfU_HsxRrV4?t=12652" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:30:52</a> | Is there any significance to the color of different shabtis? | He said the colors mostly reflect faience and material choices, with no clear special meaning attached to the color itself. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pfU_HsxRrV4?t=12976" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:36:16</a> | How did ancient Egyptians deal with overcrowding, overpopulation, and transport congestion? | He said population pressure was limited by restricted movement, distributed farming communities, famine, disease, and plague, while congestion was mostly a waterway or later cart issue. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/pfU_HsxRrV4?t=13395" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:43:15</a> | Did everyone become owned by the king after Joseph bought them with grain? | He said the Hyksos likely gained land control and vassalage for taxation, not outright ownership of people as slaves. |
